title: Agent Approval Boundary Design
summary: Decide where an agent can act alone, where it must ask, and how traces make autonomy debuggable.
whenToUseIt: Use this before exposing tool-using or action-taking agents to real users or production systems.
prerequisites: A list of tools or systems the agent could touch

steps.title: Map possible actions
steps.description: List every read, write, message, API call, and state change the agent could perform.

steps.title: Classify risk
steps.description: Mark each action as safe autonomous, supervised, or forbidden based on user trust, reversibility, and business impact.

steps.title: Define approval checkpoints
steps.description: Choose the exact moments where the system pauses for a human, requests confirmation, or shows the proposed action.

steps.title: Require trace visibility
steps.description: Ensure every tool call, intermediate decision, and failure path is logged with enough context to debug after the fact.
steps.aiPrompt: Identify which of these agent actions need approval, rollback support, or a hard deny based on risk.

steps.title: Write the fallback path
steps.description: Document what happens when the agent is uncertain, blocked, or unable to complete the task safely.

expectedOutcome: An agent workflow with explicit autonomy boundaries, approval gates, and traceable failure modes.
commonMistakes: Granting write access before the team has evidence loops
Hiding the agent's reasoning and tool activity from reviewers
Assuming a user-facing disclaimer is a sufficient safety control
